item.title: AI and the new Mechanical Turk
item.description: The evolution of the technology offers fresh opportunities for hoodwinkers and charlatans

item.title: The political fast lane keeps making the same scandal mistake
item.description: A story might be reported to no great acclaim for months before it finally blows up in the public consciousness

item.title: Wall Street ‘Super Tuesday’ gives a flavour of earnings to come
item.description: Fortunately, the fact that everyone is reporting at once matters ever less

item.title: Why the world should copy Trump for once
item.description: The US is offering $1,000 to fund investment accounts for children born during the president’s second term

item.title: Entrepreneurial aristocrats fight decline with YouTube channels and Halloween events
item.description: Chatelaines of English ancestral homes are coming up with unorthodox ideas — not to everyone’s taste

item.title: The deceptive promises of America’s democratic socialists
item.description: The way forward for the party is not to swing to the left

item.title: Why Mastercard is playing the polite guest in the UK
item.description: The card giant is considering making a strategic exit — by selling a majority stake in payments subsidiary Vocalink

item.title: Burnham will struggle to exorcise Labour’s historic weakness
item.description: History shows us that the party will default to over-optimism and spending money that isn’t there

item.title: US quantum computing needs a national buyer
item.description: Washington’s demand signal could do for the technology what early defence contracts did for silicon

item.title: Why China still believes in the power of rote memorisation
item.description: The art of learning ancient texts by heart has persisted through decades of cultural transformation

item.title: Trump’s plan for Europe is falling apart
item.description: The US president has a genius for alienating existing and potential allies

item.title: A bold, unpopular Budget could pay off
item.description: Getting big and controversial reforms out of the way can reap dividends for governments closer to election time

item.title: A global supermarket chain remains retail’s great white whale
item.description: Tesco’s retreat is sensible

item.title: In defence of . . . prediction markets?
item.description: Turning everything into a wager can get ugly, but has its upsides

item.title: Should the US redistribute or pre-distribute wealth?
item.description: Political stability in the US requires it — the question is, pre-distribution or redistribution?

item.title: AI is not enough to arrest China’s decline
item.description: There are other, more powerful forces weighing on the country’s economy

item.title: Asia’s capital surplus favours the safest winners
item.description: Investors have favoured Japan and South Korea over larger domestic-focused economies

item.title: The rebrand that would be like Darth Vader donning rainbow robes and dancing at Pride
item.description: A behind-the-scenes look at the work of Rutherford Hall, critical communications strategist

item.title: Yes, peer review has problems. But Trump’s solution is dangerous
item.description: Replacing academic judgment with political alignment undermines the very engine of discovery

item.title: The great reinvention of Hunter Biden
item.description: You have to hand it to him — the man knows how to troll with the best of them

item.title: A three-way nuclear arms race
item.description: Today’s world has alarming echoes of the era before weapons controls

item.title: Why are China and India so bad at football?
item.description: Despite large populations and economies, the countries consistently fail to qualify for the World Cup

item.title: Quantum is exciting, but watch out for the Schrödinger’s cat bounce
item.description: The ‘fault tolerant’ computer — one that is acceptably error-free — is still some time away

item.title: The EU has ways out of its budget trap
item.description: Side agreements and special purpose funds could bypass the usual trench warfare

item.title: It pays to kiss up and kick down
item.description: But treating colleagues badly might not be quite as helpful as some of the worst offenders think
